text: CEFNLLYS, or KEVENLEECE, a parish of Radnorshire. Pop. 379. Pop. of parliamentary borough 26.
CEFNNPOST, a township in the p. of Llanfihangel-Glyn-y-Myfyr, Merionethshire. Pop. 76.
CEFNPENNAR, a hamlet in the p. of Aberdare, Glamorganshire. Pop. 834. CEFNRHOS ISA and CEFNRHOS UCHA, townships in the p. of Towyn, Merionethshire. Pop. of C. Isa, 633; of C. Ucha, 93.
CEFNYBEREN, a township in the p. of Kerry, Montgomeryshire. Pop. 83.
CEFNYR-OWEN, a township in the p. of Dolgelly, Merionethshire. Pop. 66.
CEFYN, a township in the p. of Cilcen, Flintshire. Pop. 302.

CEGA, a river of Spain, which takes its rise in the Sierra Somo, about 15 m. E of Segovia; runs N and NW and W through the prov. of that name; receives the Piron on the l. on the confines of the prov. of Valladolid, and falls into the Duero after a total course of about 75 m.

CEGAMA, a small town of Spain, in Guipuzcoa, partido of Azpeitia, 40 m. S of Bilbao. Pop. 1,480. The celebrated Carlist general, Zumalacarregui, died here on the 24th of June 1835, after being wounded at Bilbao.
CEGLIE, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra d'Otranto, district and 29 m. W of Brindisi, on a hill. Pop. 9,570. It contains several churches, and has two annual fairs.
CEGLOW, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Mazovie, obwod and 15 m. SE of Stanislawów. Pop. 250.

CEHEGIN, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 44 m. WNW of Murcia, partido of Caravaca, between the Quipar and Argoy. Pop. 9,605. It is of considerable antiquity; and contains an ancient castle, a church, an hospital, and several convents, and manufactories of paper and linen and distilleries.

CEICER-DE-MER, an island of the China sea, 48 m. from the coast of Annam, in N lat. 10° 33', E long. 108° 56'. It is 6 m. in length, and is very fertile. It abounds with edible birds' nests and ambergris. The inhabitants, who are active and industrious, carry on a considerable traffic in salted fish.
CEICER - DE - TERRE, a small island of the China sea, 11 m. from the coast of Annam, in N lat. 11° 12′, E long. 108° 50′.
CEIDIO, a parish of Carnaryonshire, 6 m. NNW of Pwllheli. Pop. 138.
CEIDIO, or Rhodogeidio, a parochial chapelry of Anglesea. Pop. 423.
CEILHES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, 45 m. WNW of Montpellier, cant. of Lunas, on the r. bank of the Orbe. Pop. 1,064.
CEILLOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Saint-Dier. Pop. 1,108.
CEINOS, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 33 m. NW of Valladolid, partido of Valoria-la- Buena.

CEIRA, or ELNAS, a river of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, which takes its rise in the Serra d'Estrella, receives the Esa on the l., and unites with the Mondego, 2 m. E of Coimbra, after a course of about 54 m. Gold is found in small quantities in its channel.
CEIRCHIOG, a parochial chapelry of Anglesea. Pop. 160.

CELA (SANTA-MARIA-DE), a town of Spain in Galicia, in the prov. and partido of Pontevedra, on the peninsula of Morrazo. Pop. 1,370. It is of considerable antiquity, and contains a parish-church and a custom-house. Fish forms the chief article of local traffic.
CELADA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 26 m. SW of Leon, partido and 2 m. SE of Astorga, near the r. bank of the Tuerto.
CELADA-DEL- CAMINO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and partido and 12 m. WSW of Burgos, near the r. bank of the Arlanzon.
CELANA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation of Bergamo.

CELANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 6 m. ENE of Avezzano, near the N bank of Lake Fucinus or Celano, and at an alt. of 2,180 above sea-level. Pop. 3,868. It II. * CEL contains several churches, and has a paper-mill. Fairs are held twice a-year.—The lake is an extensive sheet of water of an elliptical form, and is skirted by a narrow and highly cultivated plain; behind which, on two of its sides, soar some of the loftiest of the Apennines. The project has been conceived of uniting the two coasts of Naples by a canal, to be fed from this lake, and to unite the Garigliano with the Pescara.
CELANOVA, a town of Spain, in Galicia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 15 m. S of Orense, and in a valley of the same name. It contains a church, a monastery, and an hospital.

CELAYA, or Zelaya, a town of Mexico, in the dep. and 38 m. SE of Guanaxuato. Pop. about 10,000. It has a fine square, two rich convents, and two cotton manufactories. The country between this town and El Opasco is swampy, but is traversed by a splendid embankment.—Löwenstern.
CELBRIDGE, a parish and town in co. Kildare. The p. has an area of 1,843 acres. Pop. 1,559.— The town, which is partly in the p. of Donacomper, is on the Liffey, 34 m. SW of Lucan. Pop. 1,289.

CELEBES, a large island in the Eastern archipelago, lying between the parallels of 2° N, and 6° S, and the meridians of 125° and 129° 30'. It is separated from Borneo on the W by the strait of Macassar; the Molucca passage and Molucca sea separate it on the E from the Molucca archipelago; on the N, that portion of the ocean which lies betwixt it and the Sulu islands and Mindanao is frequently called the sea of Celebes; on the S it has the Flores sea, or the sea of Sunda. Its superficies has been variously estimated by different writers at from 55,000 sq. m. to 75,000 sq. m. The truth is, its figure is so singularly irregular—being not inaptly compared by Malte Brun to a star-fish from which the radiating limbs on one side only have been removed—that it would be a matter of considerable difficulty to make even an approximative estimate of its area; our charts and maps of the island, besides, are far from having attained satisfactory accuracy. The long northern arm of the island, which terminates in Cape Coffin, the extreme NE point of C., forms the N side of the deep bay or gulf of Tomini, or Gunong-Tello, opening into the Molucca passage. A central and less-prolonged arm, terminating in Cape Vetch or Talyabo, forms the S side of the gulf of Tomini, and the N side of Tolo or Tomeku gulf opening into the Molucca sea. The SE arm of the island separates Tolo gulf from the deep gulf of Boni, opening into the Flores sea; while the S or SW arm forms the W side of that gulf, and has on its W side the wide S opening of the strait of Macassar. A line drawn W by S from Cape Coffin to the intersection of the 120th meridian with the equator; and thence S along that meridian to the extremity of the S arm of the island, would measure 670 m.; but a circle whose diam. is 100 m. would circumscribe what may be considered the body or nucleus of the island, touching the strait of Macassar on the W, and the head of each of the three deep gulfs above named. The average breadth of the several arms of the island does not appear to exceed 60 m. The coasts are lined and sprinkled with coral reefs and patches. Their total extent is nearly 2,500 m. Physical features.] The central part of C. is represented to be a high mountainous tract, from which mountain-ranges radiate into the four arms or peninsular projections of the island; but extensive grassy plains, free of timber, and clothed with short rich grass, are said to be found in different quarters. Beheld from the sea, the mountain-ridges present a bold outline in many quarters; while their sloping sides are diversified with wood and patches of cleared land. In the N limb of the island are several volcanic mountains in a state of occasional activity. At the S extremity, near the Dutch settlement of Bonthian, the mountain of Lampi-Betong attains an alt. of 8,000 ft. above sea-level. The coasts generally are bold and broken. The mountains are clothed with timber and bushes of lower growth; but forest - wood is comparatively scarce, except in the central parts of the island. Above the region of the cocoa-nuts and palms, the mountains are covered with coarse tall grass and ferns. The surface is watered by numerous mountain-torrents; but from its peculiar conformation, C. cannot present any rivers of long course. The largest is the Chiurani, which, rising in the central nucleus of mountains, in the Wadjo country, flows S into the bay of Boni. On the N coast the river Boli is navigable for some miles, and is said to have a long and winding course. Climate and productions.] The deep broad gulfs amidst which this island lies diffused as it were, and the constant circulation of the sea-breeze amongst the mountains and valleys, render the temperature of C. comparatively mild for its equatorial situation. The heat is likewise moderated by the copious rains which fall here. The E monsoon lasts from May to November; the opposite one prevails throughout the rest of the year. Slight shocks of earthquakes are sometimes felt; and violent storms occasionally visit the island. The S peninsula is reported to be the most healthy.—The climate and soil are highly favourable to agricultural industry, of which rice, cotton, and sugar are the principal objects; maize, cassava, potatoes, coffee, and tobacco are likewise cultivated; and the sago tree is extensively reared, and contributes largely to the support of the native tribes. The mango, the guava, the jack, the plantain, and a great variety of fruits and vegetables, are cultivated. Agriculture, however, is in a backward condition, and the cereals raised in C. do not appear to suffice for its consumption, as rice is imported every year from Java. The natives of C. are more commercial and seafaring in their habits than agricultural; and as the coasts abound with fish, they draw their subsistence more easily from fishing than the cultivation of the soil.-C. abounds in game, deer, and wild hogs; and appears to be remarkably free from the larger beasts of prey. Horses, cows, buffaloes, goats, and sheep, are likewise numerous. The Macassar horses, large in the bone and stronglimbed, are esteemed; and many hundreds of them are annually exported to Java, where they are esteemed as hunters. The cattle are small, and have a hump on the back. A species of urus is said to be found in the forests in the interior; and the babyrussa inhabits the higher regions. Among the wilder tenants of the forest are numerous herds of dusky-coloured baboons. Game abounds; and paroquets, lourikeets, the cream-coloured pigeon of Borneo, and various other species of pigeons, are numerous. Population.] The native pop. of C. is composed of several distinct races, speaking distinct languages. The Macassar language is spoken by some tribes in the SW limb. In the central parts of the island, the Mandharese is extensively spoken. The Bugi language prevails around the gulf of Boni, and is perhaps that most extensively spoken in the island. The Manados, or Menados, who occupy the E extremity of the most northern limb, have also a distinct language.—The institutions of the Bugis are original and peculiar, and present the strange anomaly of a feudal nobility, with freeholders of the soil of all ranks, and a limited monarchy. The Bugi states are 5 in number. Luwu, the oldest and the most extensive, lies around the head of the gulf of Boni, and stretches down the E side of the gulf. The state of Wadjo or Wajo is the freest and most interesting of the five; though the practice of its chiefs, a recent traveller reports, is greatly at variance with the theory of its government. There are six great hereditary chiefs,three civil and three military. These chiefs elect a monarch or chief magistrate; the right of deposing him is likewise in their hands. Below these chiefs is a council of 40 arongs or nobles; but in cases of emergency or necessity, a meeting is convened of nobles and freemen who debate on questions without deciding. The interests of the freemen are guarded by three pangwas chosen by themselves from their own class, and the power of these tribunes of the people is considerable. They alone possess the power of summoning the people to war, of calling the council, &c. The nobles live on their own petty territories or estates, surrounded by followers and slaves; and all possess the right of making war on each other in case of quarrel or dispute. "Bold, enterprising, and hardy, sensible to insult and always ready to resent it, keen traders, and good colonists, the Bugis are highly interesting. Yet it is to be regretted that all the evils of European interference are fomenting amongst them. A contested succession to a country called Se-Dindring has been the origin of their present disorganized state. The Dutch supporting one side, have created jealousy and distrust among the native kingdoms, and will avail themselves of the opportunity to enlarge their own possessions and injure our trade. Their last act was to intimate to the Wajo chiefs that in case of their interfering in the Se-Dindring succession, Dutch cruisers would be sent into the bay to stop their proas. These proas trade with Singapore. Pare Parè, a fine port on the W coast, belonging to Se- Dindring, will be ceded to them by their rajah of Se- Dindring, who is dependent on their support. Where the Dutch tread, commerce withers, and the English are excluded. They are pressing Boni, and it is likely that their object is a military post in the bay. The Bugis trade is so important to Singapore, that it is probable its loss might attract some attention." [Calcutta Journal, Sept. 1840.] Among the Bugis nations, the Wajo Bugis are the most distinguished for maritime enterprise and commercial spirit. A writer in the Singapore Chronicle informs us that the original country of Wajo is nearly in the centre of Celebes, on the N banks of an extensive fresh-water lake about 24 m. in breadth. The outlet from this lake is a river which falls into the bay of Boni, and which is navigable for boats of 20 tons burthen. "Such is the original country of this remarkable race, where they live under a true federal aristocracy, the inferior chiefs being elected from particular families, each being chosen by the rest, and holding his office during good behaviour. The people of Wajo pay neither land-tax, nor any other species of contribution, being exempt even from imposts on trade of any sort or description. The inferior chiefs support themselves from their own domains, and other private revenue; and the arumatua, or president of the union, alone obtains three days' personal services in the year, one in ploughing-time, one in seed-time, and one in the time of harvest. The Wajo men are perfectly free to go abroad and return at pleasure. It is the pride of a free man of Wajo, 'that no chief or prince can shut the gates of the country against him;' and they often dwell with satisfaction upon this advantage, repeating the expression. The other governments of C. are more arbitrary, and far less favourable to industry. Under them a tithe of the gross produce of the land is paid * to the sovereign as a tax; corvées are frequent, military service oppressive, and no subject can quit the country without the will of the government, in itself a formidable instrument both of oppression and taxation. The advantages of the free form of government now described, notwithstanding the turbulence and anarchy to which it is occasionally liable, are the true sources of the industry and enterprise of the Wajos. Actuated by these, this tribe has colonized in almost every maritime country of the Archipelago, where they preserve their original manners and habits, while their commercial voyages extend from Manila to Acheen, and from Siam to New Guinea; it being observed that the parent-country is by no means that which now carries on the most extensive foreign trade. It is singular that this people should be the sole native carriers to the Archipelago; all the other tribes confining themselves to mere coasting voyages. When, for example, we hear that the islands of Bali and Lombok; Macassar, Mandar, and Kaili, in C.; Gresik, in Java; Basir and Cuti, in Borneo; and similar places, carry on a trade with the new settlement of Singapore, this always means the trade of the Bugis of Wajo; for we here never see a trading native of Bali or Lombok, nor of the greater number of the other places, although their commodities be brought to us in abundance, and ours be conveyed to them in return. The most correct list which we have been able to obtain of the number of the Wajo prahus carrying on foreign trade in 1824 was as follows: Prahus. Sumbawa, 40 East coast of Borneo, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Mandar in Celebes, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Boniratti, a small island between Celebes and Sumbawa, 50 Pari Pari in Celebes, 10 Bali and Lombok, 50 Java, 50 Flores, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . These make in all a total of 786 prahus. Their burthen is commonly from 20 to 60 tons, and the average value of their cargoes cannot well be estimated at less than 4,000 dollars each. A few rich ones, chiefly carrying birds' nests and tortoise-shell, have imported cargoes into Singapore valued at 30,000 dollars. These cargoes vary according to the countries from which the Bugis sail, but the details are too extensive to find room in a short essay of this nature. The following brief recital, however, will convey some general notions in regard to them. The traders of Wajo Proper chiefly export cotton cloths, manufactured by the women of their own country; the traders of Bali, Lombok, and Java, export cotton fabrics, the manufactures of those countries, with rice and oil; the traders of Mandar export oil, rice, and, within the last year, some coffee; those of Kaili export a great deal of gold; those of Macassar, cloths, tortoise-shell, and sea-slug; the traders of the east coast of Borneo deal in esculent birds' nests and tortoise-shell; and those of Flores in the same commodities. We may here remark, that the Bugis are only carriers and general merchants, and have very little share in the collection or preparation of the articles which compose their cargoes. Spices and birds of Paradise, for example, are supplied to them by the negroes of Ceram and New Guinea; and sea-slug, tortoise-shell, and birds' nests, by a singular amphibious race called Bajao, who live a wandering life in their boats, without any other home or habitation, devoting their existence to fishing. The commodities which the Bugis carry away from the European settlements consist of opium, gunpowder, fire-arms, iron, coarse Bengal | for shipping and trade, whereby an unequal system <del>゚゙</del>゚゙゚゚゙゙゚゚゙゚゚゙゙゙゙゙゚゚゙゚゙゚゚゚゙゚゙゚ヹ゚゚ヹ゚ヹ゚ヹ゚ヹ cotton goods, Europe chintzes, and some broadcloths, raw silk, Chinese pottery, Siamese and Chinese culinary utensils, and Javanese tobacco." Dutch territories and influence.] The Dutch have two residencies on the island: Macassar on the SW, and Menado on the NE. The territories pertaining to the former are not very extensive; but the latter embraces, nominally at least, a considerable portion of the northern half of the island, of which we are enabled to furnish considerable details in this article. The Dutch have always made it the basis of their treaties with the native states of C. that they should abstain from all external commerce; but they have of late years adopted a wiser policy, and by throwing open their port of Macassar, have endeavoured to cut off the trade of Singapore with the natives of C. In a memorial from the Singapore board-of-trade, dated November 1847, it is stated that the opening of the port of Macassar has only been made a pretence for further restrictions and more entire exclusion of British trade with native powers or states under Dutch influence or control; that the Dutch authorities at Macassar have declared that port is the only place on the island of C. open to foreign trade, and that they threatened with seizure and confiscation any British trading vessel proceeding, not only to ports on those parts of C. which the Dutch have taken possession of, but even to ports of native states on that island which are still independent, or with which the Dutch are only connected by treaties, and to which, by the 3d and 4th articles or the treaty of 1824, British traders have a clear right of access. The memorialists further assert that the treaty between the Netherlands government and the sultan of Jambie, a native state on the island of Sumatra, dated the 15th of Dec., 1834, which so far back as the 31st May, 1841, was declared by Viscount Palmerston to be at variance in a number of its provisions with the treaty of 1824, and to "have a direct tendency to inflict on British commercial interests the injury against which the treaty of 1824 was intended to guard," is still maintained in full force by the Netherlands government; and that under the provisions of the 8th and other articles of the said treaty, the regulations for shipping and trade of Netherlands India have been put in operation at the port of Moeara-Kompeh in Jambie, by a recent besluit of the governor-general of Netherlands India, by which unequal duties are imposed on goods imported and exported under the Netherlands flag, and from and to Netherlands India ports, and under the flags to and from the ports of other powers respectively. The memorialists further represent that for many years past a very considerable trade has been carried on by British subjects with the island of Bali; but that the proceedings of the Netherlands government in June 1846, against two of the most powerful rajahs of Bali, resulting in the permanent station of an armed Dutch force in the country, and the exaction of treaties acknowledging the supremacy of the Netherlands over these states, have produced an injurious effect on the trade between Singapore and Bali, and that the British trade with Bali is in danger of extinction by the incorporation of that island with Java, a measure which they believe will ultimately follow the present proceedings of the Netherlands India government. They add that the treaties from time to time made by the Netherlands India government with native powers in the Indian archipelago are invariably intended to affect British trade in one of two ways: first, by obliging the native rulers to forbid foreign trade, and thereby totally excluding British subjects; and secondly, by allowing the introduction into the states of such native powers of the Dutch regulations of duties is imposed; -in both cases, they argue, the freedom and equality of trade guaranteed by the treaty of 1824 being destroyed. Macassar.] Macassar, on the W coast of the S arm of the island, in the district of Fort Rotterdam, is the principal establishment of the Dutch in C., and the seat of government for the Dutch residencies or districts in the island, and likewise for those in Sumbava and Salair. The European pop. of the town itself, exclusive of the garrison, does not exceed 800; and that of the whole district 15,000; but the neighbouring territory under the native rajah or chief of Macassar contains a pop. of 65,000. Menado and its dependencies.] The Dutch settlement at Menado, on the NE extreme of C., is of more importance than Macassar. The N coast being less favourably situated for commerce than the S, more attention is paid by the natives to agriculture; and two or three ships of about 800 tons now annually collect cargoes, chiefly of coffee, on this coast, with which they proceed direct to Holland. [Earl.] The districts stretching from the bottom of the bay of Tomini to the extremity of the N arm, besides many islands in the bay and several districts in the peninsula of Taliyabo, are claimed by the Dutch, as dependencies of their recently established residence at Menado. And of these we are enabled to furnish a somewhat minute account from an article in the Journal of the Indian Archipelago for Oct. 1848. It appears that this part of C. is divided into the countries of Gurontalo, Limbotte, Boni, Bolangen, and Andagili, with Antingola. These five countries are commonly designated by the general name of Lima pahalla. The country is mountainous, with numerous narrow valleys, and a multitude of small rivers. There are some large marshes on the sea-coast. If not volcanic itself, yet the district is undoubtedly connected with volcanoes, and therefore suffers from strong earthquakes. The breadth of the peninsula is from 22 to 40 m. The climate is very warm, but not oppressive nor unhealthy. Average of heat from September to November: 6 o'clock 69° Morning Morning Fevers prevail only among the labourers of the gold mines in Sumalatte, Andagili and Pagowat. A disease of the eyes, which is frequent at Bolila, is said to be caused by the too frequent use of the warm baths which exist there. The northern part of the island, here under review, is traversed by a ridge of mountains which on an average do not exceed the height of 2,000 ft., with the exception of a few points, viz., Kabili, near Boni, 4,000 ft.; Andagili, near the town of Andagili, 4,200 ft.; and Gulahatto, near Sumalatte, 5,400 ft. The mountains of the whole chain are steep, especially to the north. The longitudinal valleys are few; they are mostly transverse. There are therefore scarcely any plains: only three such are formed, viz., 1st., the plain of Gurontalo and Limbotte, in length from W to E 16 m., and breadth 5 to 7 m., which appears to have been originated by the retreating of the sea, being only a few feet above its level; 2d., at Pagowat; and 3d., the island, only a few are navigable for boats to a short distance. The rivers which flow into the bay of Tomini are, 1st., Gurontalo, being a discharge of 404 S, receiving on its left the Bothair and Bolangen, and attaining greater depth after being joined from the E not far from its mouth, by the Boni. Small boats are able to ascend to the lake. 2d., Pagogama, which runs from SW to SE, has its sources upon and near Gulahatte, and is narrow, deep cut, and with many rapids. Small boats go up as far as Tongabon. 3d., Pagowat, an unimportant stream, is formed by the confluence of the Batu-Dulango and Talo-Duyuno. 4th., Milandao, running S, from Gunong Bamwah. It is of considerable breadth, and is navigable far up; it is rich in gold, and not far from it are the mines of Wankahulu. 5th., Malisipat, the most considerable of them all, runs S. with many side creeks. It takes its rise from the mountains of Baol, and constitutes the frontier between Moutton and Gurontalo. Among the northerly streams deserve to be named: 1st., Andagili, descending from the mountain of Kasudipang, rich in gold, but otherwise inconsiderable. 2d., Quandam, descending from Gunong-Pintu-Besar, having a crooked course, and navigable for a short distance. 3d., Lamatta, from the slope of Gunong-Marinsu, with waterfalls and rapids. 4th., Sumalatte, having its sources on the Gulahatto. Near it are the gold mines of Sumalatte. The only inland lake is in the plain of Limbotte, near the town of the same name; lying from W to E, in length 4 m.; and in breadth between Limbotte and Bolemo, 2 m. There are some marshes around the lake of Limbotte, and some on the N coast between Quandam and Sumalatte, and at the mouth of the Malisipat.—There is a large group of islands on the N coast opposite the mouth of the Quandam, and in the bay of Tomini, among which may be mentioned as of importance for commerce: Una, Togian, Malinche, Walia, and Matabella, upon whose large sand-banks and cliffs a great quantity of trepang and turtles are collected by the Bajorese. The coast on the N presents everywhere sands and cliffs; it is much flatter to the S.— There are some paths for travellers on horseback between Gurontalo, Bolangen, Limbotte, Boni, and Bolemo; also some paths over the mountains from Limbotte to Quandam, one of which leads right over nong-Tiliule and Marinsu at a height of 1,850 ft. These paths are travelled on horseback, though they are highly dangerous. There are communications between Boni and Kayu-Dipang; as also between Bolangen and Andagili; but these are only to be passed on foot. The productions of the above districts are gold, wax, pearls, turtles, trepang, cinnamon, coffee, cacao, birds' nests, horses, kapok, or cotton from the Bombyx pentrandum, kapas, or cotton from the Gossypium herbaceum, sarongs, salt, wood, and particularly some excellent kinds of iron-wood. There are probably no aborigines within the above districts, or only a mixture of these with Bugis and immigrants from Ternate and Tidore. The number of the pop. is small in proportion to the extent of ground. It is difficult to form a correct estimate of them, as the inhabitants are scattered here and there. Late accounts give, for 1. Gurontalo, In this account Prigi and Mutton are not included, as the officers of government are withdrawn from water from the lake of Limbotte, flowing E and then | thence. Prigi numbers about 3,000 and Mutton 1,750 souls. The revenue of government is derived almost | entirely from the leasing of opium farms, and the profit from gold dust, which, according to contract, the different districts must sell to the government for a stipulated price (viz., 14 rupees in copper for a tael, and 16 copper rupees for gold from Gurontalo). The whole amount of gold dust, which the Lima pahallas have stipulated to furnish annually in this way to government, is the weight of 1,000 Spanish dollars, or 500 taels. This amount the five districts have distributed among themselves in proportion to the amount of their pop.; and some districts, not having rich gold mines themselves, are permitted to derive their complements from the mines of the other districts. Their separate allotments stand thus: Dollars. Gurontalo furnishes the weight of 400 300 Limbotte do. do. 80 Boni do. do. 70 Bolangen do. do. Andagili, with Antingola, 120 do. 30 Bolemo do. do. 1,000 If we rate one tael of the gold dust at 80 Java rupees, the revenue of the government will consist of the following items: Rupees. The expenses of government may be taken on a fair estimation to be the following: Rupees. 1. Purchase money of the 500 taels of gold dust, 15,500 2. Salaries of an intendant with his clerk. of 50 soldiers under the command of a lieutenant, and of 10,000 the police peons, about 25,500 The commerce and shipping, on the whole, is very trifling; and is conducted, with the exception of two or three coasters, solely by the Paduwakans or Bugis prahus, under the command of Arabs, Bugis, and Chinese. The commerce chiefly consists in the exchange of articles. The imports consist of European goods: cloth, linen, gold and silver galloons, leather, iron goods, glass, earthenware, silk, and sugar. The Bugis further import from Singapore gunpowder, guns, opium, and from Menado and Ternate rice and sago. The exports comprise gold, sarongs, cotton cloth, &c., a kind of paper, kapok, kapas in a raw state and worked, baskets, wax, cinnamon, wood, turtles, trepang, birds' nests, and horses. The Dutch have here merely a civile gezaghebber, who has to report to the resident of Menado. The native government is conducted as follows:-Each district has a chief, with the title of raja, who is recognised and appointed by the Dutch government. In Gurontalo and Limbotte there are two rajas, the one called raja negri, and the other raja gobernement, the latter being introduced for the sake of equipoise against the prince. All despatches of government are first transmitted to the latter, who afterwards consults with his colleague, but the former alone is responsible for the execution of the order given. All other officers of government are appointed by the raja. These officers are the juhugus or councillors of state, and the negris or chiefs of the smaller districts. The raja gobernement enjoys everywhere, of course, the larger share of authority and respect; though the raja negri has the precedence in rank. The police belongs as well to the civile gezaghebber as to the rajas. Criminal cases are brought before the landraad or provincial court, consisting of the civile gezaghebber, the commanding lieutenant, and one or two rajas, with other chiefs. The division of the population into castes is uniform in all districts. It was probably introduced from Ternate; and is strongly adhered to, having been established for centuries. Three classes are chiefly distinguished: 1st, bobatus, or patricians; 2d, balla, the middle class and peasantry; 3d, budak, or slaves. All the higher offices and posts of honour are monopolized by the bobatu. From them is chosen the raja, from the oldest families; the juhugu, or counsellors or ministers; the capitan laut, or admiral; the walopulu, or chiefs of larger negris or communities; the marsaoli, or chiefs of villages, who in this capacity are at the same time representatives or tribunes of the people. They are chosen exclusively by the balla and the mangofuli, or slaves of the community. The marsaoli have thus become the lawgiving body, while the raja has the executive. In former times the marsaoli had the right to appoint and to dethrone the raja; and even to try him for life, if he outraged the law. Their persons are in a certain measure sacred. With the balla rests all the burthen of taxes, tribute, &c. Their deputies are the kimelaha kiri, who choose the marsaoli and constitute his council. The slaves form the largest portion of the population, and their condition is not oppressive. There are several subdivisions: 1st, the mangofuli, or mangohuli, who are very numerous, and not properly slaves, excepting as regards some feudal services for the community, which they have to discharge, and which are transmitted from father to son. A great number of them were recently incorporated with the balla. Their deputies are the kimelaha kanan. 2d, Buda pusaka, or slaves by inheritance. These live entirely with their master and his family, and work for them, having become slaves originally by debt and inability to pay it. 3d, the Buda, or common slaves, captives made in former wars, and their descendants. They may be sold or given away, but not into other countries and provinces.—The princes and aristocracy imitate the European dress, with the exception of the head-dress, considering very justly the turban or handkerchief much more convenient. The middle classes dress entirely in the Bugis fashion, and display great luxury, wearing generally silks and costly kinds of cloth, richly bordered and covered with gold and silver laces. Their sarongs, of the finest kind, are fastened round the waist by belts, which are sometimes richly ornamented with gold and diamonds. Instances are known where 120 dollars have been paid for the wooden scabbard of a kris, and common rifles are exchanged for gold dust to the amount of 300 and 400 rupees. Among such articles of luxury, the apparatus for opium smoking has a prominent place, the pipes being of embossed gold, silver, or ivory. The dress of the lower classes for the men consists of short trousers of cloth; the upper part of the body is generally naked, but jackets with short sleeves are sometimes used. A sarong, folded in the manner of salendangs, which serves as a cloak in cold and rainy weather and during the night, is worn round the waist. The head-dress is either a small cap made of rattan, or a handkerchief, negligently wound round the head, with one end hanging loosely down. For protection from rain and sunshine, a large hat, made of sago leaves, is used. This class is mostly armed with the kris, tombak, and parang. The dress of females of the higher classes, like those in Java, consists of transparent stuffs with gold and silver textures, and ornaments of gold, silver, and diamonds. In domestic retirement all splendour and ornaments are laid aside, and filth and sloth are the order of the day. Nothing but a sarong of coarse and generally dark coloured cloth is bound round the waist, and a piece of black crape is sometimes thrown over the shoulders to cover the breast. Sometimes the females wear short jackets, white, yellow, green, or blue; but this is not frequent, as it is generally the dress of slaves. The poorer classes are content with a few rags of the bark of trees. The dwellings of the Ballas are miserable huts of bamboo, standing on high posts, and consisting of one room which at the same time is lodging, kitchen, and store. The furniture consists of some weapons, mats, siri-boxes, a pot for cooking jagong or maize, and a cushion, i. e. saddle for the horse. The occupations of the men are the cultivation of their small farm, fishing, and digging for gold. Every spare moment is devoted to opium-smoking, cock-fighting, gambling, and sleeping. The women, besides all the domestic labours, have to gather in and to sell the produce of their farm, to weave cloth, and to nurse and attend to the children. Here, as well as almost everywhere throughout the Archipelago, it seems the custom that the wife should support the husband, and hence it is that polygamy can exist without much jealousy. Agriculture is here entirely in its infancy. Large tracts of the most fertile ground lie entirely waste. The plough was introduced after the war in Java (1830), the auxiliary troops from Gurontalo having become acquainted with it when there; but it is still not much used. Coffee and sugar-cane they can scarcely ever be induced to cultivate, as even those necessaries of Indian life, betel nut, siri, &c., are so little attended to that they are invariably unreasonably dear. There are even instances of fruit trees having been cut down, in order to save the trouble of watching and reaping the fruit. Cinnamon, kapok, and kapas, although in great abundance, are only so far attended to and used as absolute necessity compels, and those valuable kinds of wood in which the forests abound, and which could be furnished to any extent, are all neglected. - Their manufactured articles consist of-1st, Sarongs of kapok or kapas, differing in fineness and beauty. 2d, Cloths made from the threads of white European linen, which is ripped up, the thread detached, beautifully dyed, and then woven again. 3d, Kabayas or bajus, and kerchiefs, fine and durable. 4th, Yarn or twine of kapas and kapok. 5th, Articles of gold and silver, neat but without particular taste, mostly siri-boxes, girdles, bracelets, combs, pins, handles of krises, and the implements for opium-smoking. 6th, Articles and carvings in wood, few and unimportant.

CELENZA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Citra, district and 18 m. SSW of Vasto, on the l. bank of the Trigno. Pop. 1,834.—Also a town, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Capitanate, district and 27 m. WSW of San Severo. Pop. 3,840. It contains 3 convents, and has an annual fair.
CELERE, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 19 m. WNW of Viterbo, and 6 m. SW of Lake Bolsena, on an affluent of the Fiora.
CELERINA, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, Ober Engadin, on the l. bank of the Inn.
CELERIN-LE-GE'NE', a village of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. and 6 m. N of Montfort. The environs contain some iron-mines.
CELETTES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. and 5 m. SE of Blois, near the r. bank of the Beuvron. Pop. 1,027. It contains a manufactory of sugar from beet-root.
CELICO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 6 m. ENE of Cosenza. Pop. 1,660. It contains 2 convents.
CELIGNY, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 4 m. SW of Nion, and 10 m. N of Geneva.
CELLA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Es- * CEL tremadura, comarca and 5 m. W of Alcobaca, at the entrance of the Alcoa with the Atlantic. Pop. 900. See also ZELLA.
CELLAMARE, or CELLAM-MARE, a village of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, district and 7 m. SE of Bari. Pop. 592.
CELLAND-LE-GRAND, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Brecey. Pop. 1,032.
CELLAR-DYKES, a fishing-village in Fifeshire, in the p. of Kilrenny, adjoining Easter Anstruther, but united as a burgh with Kilrenny. Pop. 1,486.
CELLAS, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. of Huesca, partido and 8 m. NW of Barbastro.

CELLE, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 4 m. E of Dinant. It contains an ancient castle, supposed to have been founded by Pepin d'Héristal.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, district and 21 m. SW of Campobasso. Pop. 4,361.—See also Zelle.
CELLE'. See SELLE'.

CELLE, or Zell, a town of Hanover, in the gov. and 50 m. SSW of Luneburg, and 23 m. NE of Hanover, on the Aller, at the confluence of the Fuse, in N lat. 52° 37′ 3″. Pop. 11,000. It is enclosed by walls and a ditch, and has 3 suburbs, and a royal castle and park in which is the mausoleum of Queen Matilda. It contains a Reformed, a Catholic, and 5 Lutheran churches, an orphan-asylum, 2 hospitals, a gymnasium, a medical school, a college, a lunatic asylum; and has manufactories of cloth, paper, wax-candles, starch, &c., and a saw-mill, a tile-work, and numerous breweries.
CELLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Marcellat. Pop. 1,011.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et- Marne, cant. of Coulommiers. Pop. 1,134.
CELLE-BRUERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cher, cant. and 4 m. NW of Saint- Amand. Pop. 850. The environs contain quarries of freestone.
CELLE-DUNOISE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Creuse, cant. of Dun-le-Palleteau, 13 m. NNW of Gueret. It possesses manufactories of linen.
CELLEFRONIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of Mansle, on the l. bank of the Son, 15 m. SE of Ruffec, and 23 m. NE of Angoulême. Pop. 2,043.
CELLE-LEVECAULT, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. of Lusignan, 15 m. SSW of Poitiers, on the Veune. Pop. 1,547.
CELLE-NEUVE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant., com., and 2 m. W of Montpellier. Pop. 640.
CELLENT, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 35 m. NNW of Barcelona, partido and 11 m. NE of Manresa, on the l. bank of the Llobregat. pop. 1,200. It possesses manufactories of iron-wire.
CELLERFELD. See ZELLERFELD.

CELLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariège, cant. and 6 m. SE of Foix. Pop. 893. It contains some iron-works.-Also a commune in the dep. of Aube, cant. of Mussy-sur-Seine. Pop. 1,030. -Also a commune in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Montagrier. Pop. 1,651.—Also a commune in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Saint-Remy, 4 m. E of Thiers. Pop. 3,060.—Also a commune in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Allanche, on the r. bank of the Alagnon, 4 m. from Murat. Pop. 725 .- Also a canton, commune, and town in the dep. of Deux- Sevres, arrond. of Melle. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,083; in 1841, 10,439. The town is on the Belle, 4 m. NW of Melle. Pop. 1,479. · / + / P / P / P / P / P / P / P / P / P It has some manufactories of woollen fabrics.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Raonl'Etape, on the l. bank of the Plaine, 12 m. N of Saint Die. Pop. 1,525.—Also a canton, commune, and village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 10 m. NNE of Tournay, on an affluent of the Schelde. Pop. 2,692. It possesses manufactories of linen, and lint is grown in the environs.—Also a village in the same prov. and cant. of Seneffe, 8 m. NNW of Charleroi, on the r. bank of the Pieton. Pop. 1,336.
CELLE - SOUS - MORET (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Moret, on the r. bank of the Seine, 8 m. E of Fontainebleau. Pop. 284. It contains an ancient castle.
CELLE - SAINT - CYR (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Saint- Julien-du-Sault. Pop. 1,206.

CELLETTES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. and 6 m. SE of Blois, near the r. bank of the Beuvron. Pop. 980. It possesses manufactories of sugar from beet-root and potatoflour.
CELLIER (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Ligne, 10 m. SW of Ancenis, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 2,167.
CELLIEU, a village of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. and 4 m. W of Rive-de-Gier. Pop. 860. It contains manufactories of nails.

CELLINO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 12 m. SE of Teramo and 7 m. W of Atri, on a hill. Pop. 1,492. Fairs are held twice a-year.—Also a town in the prov. of the Terra-d'Otranto, district and 17 m. NW of Lecce, on the road to Bari. Pop. 800.
CELLIO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. of Val Sesia, 6 m. SE of Varallo. Pop. 2,300.
CALLORIGO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 33 m. NW of Logrono, and partido of Haro-y-sus-Barrios.
CELLULE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. and 4 m. N of Riom, on the r. bank of the Morge. Pop. 2,027.
CELLWS, a township in the p. of Llanbadarnfawr, Radnorshire. Pop. 194.
CELONE, a river of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanate, which takes its rise in the Apennines, 9 m. WSW of Troja, passes near Foggia, and after a total course of 39 m. falls into the Candelaro.

CELORICO, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca and 12 m. W of Guarda, situated on an eminence, at the base of the Serra d'Estrella, and near the Mondego. Pop. 1,800. It is a place of considerable strength, and is defended by a castle. It contains a collegiate and 2 parish churches, and a woollen-spinning establishment.
CELSO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, on the Crati, 6 m. N of Bisignano.
CELSONA. See Solsona.

CELY, or CELY-EN-BIERRE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. and 9 m. SW of Melun. Pop. 605 .- The vil. consists of a single street, extending 11 m. along the bank of a small river. In the environs is a castle built by Jacques Cœur in 1400, and a fine park.
CEMBOING. See SEMBOING.
CEMMAES, a township in the p. of Llanbadrig, Anglesey, near the little harbour of Yrwlfa. Pop. in 1801, 714; in 1841, 909.
CEMMES, a parish and village of Montgomeryshire, 8 m. NW of Machynllaeth, on the Dyfi. Pop. in 1801, 777; in 1841, 935. The village of C. is included in the township of Gwernybwlch. CEN
CENAC, a commune of France, in the dep. and on the l. bank of the Dordogne, cant. of Domme, 9 m. S of Sarlat. Pop. 1,206.
CENCENIGHE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 20 m. NW of Belluno, district and 7 m. NW of Agordo, near the l. bank of the Cordovole. It has an annual fair.
CENDEJAS-DE-LA-TORRE, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 35 m. NE of Guadalajara, partido and 12 m. WSW of Siguenza.
CENDRIEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Vergt, 16 m. SSE of Périgueux. Pop. 1,058.
CENE'. See Bois-de-Cene'.

CENEDA, or CENETA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. and 40 m. N of Venice, delegation and 23 m. NNE of Treviso, on a hill on the r. bank of the Machio. Pop. in 1837, 5,057. It is defended by a citadel; and contains a cathedral, several churches, a theological seminary, and a gymnasium. It possesses manufactories of cloth and linen, and some paper-mills and tanneries; and has fairs twice a-year. In the environs are several mineral springs.

CENERE (MONTE), a mountain of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, which detaches itself from the Jöriberg and extends to the E bank of Lago Maggiore, between the Val Morobio on the N, and Val Agno on the S.
CENESELLI, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. of Polesina, district and 3 m. E of Massa, and 27 m. WSW of Rovigo. Pop. 1,286.

CENIA, a river of Spain, in Catalonia, which takes its rise on the NW side of M. Coro, in the prov. of Tarragona; runs S to the town of the same name; thence bends SSE along the confines of the prov. of Castellon-de-la-Plana; and after a total course of 33 m. falls into the Mediterranean 2 m. SE of Alcanar.—The town of C. is 60 m. SW of Tarragona, in the partido and 10 m. SW of Tortosa, on the l. bank of the river of the same name. It is very ancient, and is still surrounded by old walls. Pop. 2,043. It contains a parish church and a custom-house, and has some paper-mills.

CENICERO, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov., partido, and 13 m. W of Logrono, near the Neila. Pop. 1,302. It is of considerable antiquity; and contains a parish church, a convent, an hospital, and a custom-house. The rearing of cattle and the distillation of brandy, form the chief branches of local industry.

CENIS (MONT), or MONTE CENISIO, a mountan at the junction of the Graian and the Cottian Alps, in N lat. 45° 14', E long. 6° 45'. Alt. above sea-level, 11,457 ft. Mont C. is not comparable with the Simplon in point of picturesque beauty, especially on the Italian side, yet it is not wholly without that quality. The level plain on the summit where the hospice stands, with its beautiful little lake [alt. 6,280 ft. above sea-level], and high mountains dipping their verdant sides into the clear water, form a striking scene. The lake discharges itself by a stream called the Cenisella into the Dora-Ripuaria at Susa. A magnificent road, begun in 1803 and completed in 1810 at an expense of £300,000, leads across Mont C. from Lans-le-bourg in the valley of the Arc in Savoy, to Susa and Turin. Its highest point of elevation is 6,773 ft. above sea-level, and 2,250 ft. above Lansle-bourg; and it rises from Lans-le-bourg to the summit of the pass at the rate of 1 in 15. It is everywhere 30 ft. wide, and has 26 houses-of-refuge along it for the safety of passengers. These houses are tenanted by the workmen whose business it is to keep the road in good order; and are provided with bells, which, during the prevalence of fogs, are rung to guide the traveller from one post to another. The difficulty of constructing the road was greatest on the Piedmontese side, where, in several places, it was necessary to excavate the galleries in hard and almost precipitous granite rock. On the Savoy side the road runs over a soft limestone rock. The distance from Lans-le-bourg to Susa is about 20 m. Novalese, the last post-station before reaching Susa, is 2,741 ft. above sea-level. Although something higher than the road over the Simplon, the Cenis route is less exposed to avalanches, not being so immediately overtopped by higher summits; yet it is not entirely safe from them in the months of January and February. The new road comes once or twice in contact with the old path. Not far from the highest point is a place called La Ramasse, from which, during winter, venturous travellers coming from Piedmont, sometimes slide directly down the snowcovered steep to Lans-le-bourg, performing the journey of 2 leagues in from 7 to 10 minutes, in a sledge guided by a mountaineer. The perpendicular descent is nearly 2,000 ft.
CENIZAS, an island off the W coast of Lower California, in N lat. 30° 46', W long. 115° 50'.
CENIZATE, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 25 m. NNE of Albacete, partido of Casas-de-Ibanez. Pop. 1,096. It contains a parish church, a school, and a custom-house.
CENNES-MONESTIES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, cant. and 9 m. E of Castelnaudary. Pop. 896. It possesses some manufactories of common cloth, and has an annual fair.
CENO, a river of Italy, in the duchy of Parma, which rises at the foot of the Apennines, and falls into the Taro near Tornovo.
CENON-LA-BASTIDE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Carbon-Blanc. Pop. 1,805.
CENSEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Jura, 19 m. E of Poligny. Pop. 819. It is an entrepot for salt for export to Switzerland, and for iron; and has a considerable trade in grain and timber.

CENTALLO, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento, in the prov. and 7 m. NNE of Coni, near the r. bank of the Grana. Pop. 5,000. It contains an ancient castle, formerly the residence of the Marquis of Susa.
CENTENERA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov., partido, and 8 m. E of Guadalajara.
CENTESIMO (PONTE), a town of the States-ofthe-Church, in the leg. and 21 m. ESE of Perugia, and 5 m. S of Nocera, on the r. bank of the Topino, where crossed by the road to Foligno.
CENTINAL ISLE, a small island in the Caribbean sea, near the coast of Venezuela, in N lat. 10° 50', W long. 66° 15'.

CENTINO (PONTE), a town of the States-of-thethe leg. and 19 m. SW of Ferrara, on the E bank of the canal of the same name, and near the l. bank of the Reno, in N lat. 44° 43'. Pop. 4,500. It is surrounded by an old rampart and ditch; and contains a cathedral, and several churches and convents. It is noted as the birthplace of J. F. Barbieri, called also Le Guerchin.—The canal of C. commences 12 m. WNW of Bologna; passes S. Giovanni-in-Persiceto, where it becomes navigable, and the town of the same name; runs for 6 m. parallel to the Reno; bends E, and unites with the Pô-de-Volano at Ferrara. Its total length is 36 m.
CENTOLA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 13 m. S of Il Vallo, CEN and 56 m. SE of Salerno. Pop. 1,225. It contains a church, an abbey, and a convent.

CENTORBI, a town of Sicily, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. and 23 m. WNW of Catania, district and 20 m. ESE of Nicosia, on a rocky eminence. Pop. 4,455. This town, the ancient Centuripa, now consists of only a single street, with a church and a convent, but is surrounded by extensive ruins of its former magnificence.
CENTRAL, a township of St. Louis co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 1,924.

CENTRAL AMERICA, a term which, in a strict geographical sense, should be applied to that portion of the American continent which lies between the isthmus of Tehuantepec on the W, and the isthmus of Panama on the E; and which may itself be regarded as an immense isthmus uniting the still vaster masses of North and South America, and separating the great gulf of Mexico from the waters of the Pacific. Its form and outline are exceedingly irregular; but it probably has an area of 150,000 sq. m. The physical features of this region will be found sketched under the heads of the various states into which it is politically divided. These are Yucatan, Guatimala, Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costarica; the Mosquito territory, and the district of Belize or British Honduras; and the Mexican provinces of Chiapas and Soconusco. Between the years 1524 and 1527, Piedro Alvarado, at the head of 400 Spaniards and 4,000 Mexican auxiliaries, subdued the regions forming Central A., and founded the city of Guatimala-Viega. Under the regime of Spain, a captain-general, resident at Guatimala, administered the government of the whole country, under the viceroy of Mexico. In 1811, some manifestations of a desire of independence revealed themselves in the province of San-Salvador, and the movement soon after extended to Nicaragua. The antagonism of parties, however, aided the efforts of Spain to retain its hold on the Guatimala provinces; but on the 15th of December, 1821, they issued a declaration of independence, and convoked a congress to be held on 1st March 1822. Before the arrival of that period, however, the provisional representatives resolved on annexing their territories to the shortlived Mexican monarchy of Iturbide. The opposition of Honduras and Nicaragua to this latter measure led to war betwixt these two states and that of Guatimala, in which the latter would have been worsted, had not the Mexican general Filisola hastened to her aid, and, marching on San-Salvador, compelled the reluctant government of that province to sign an act of reunion to Guatimala. The fall of Iturbide convinced Filisola of the hopeless nature of the attempt to consolidate Central A. with Mexico. In a congress which began its sittings in the month of January 1823, the federal union of the five states of Guatimala, San-Salvador, Honduras, and Costarica, under the title of the United States of Central A., was resolved on, and a declaration to this effect was promulgated on 1st July 1823. On the 22d of November, 1824, the national assembly completed its task of framing a constitution for the new federal republic; but the provinces of Chiapas and Soconusco had before its appearance reunited themselves to Mexico. By the constitution of 1824 the federal government was vested in a president to be chosen every five years, and a congress composed of a senate and a house of representatives. Every state was to send 2 members to the senate, and 1 member for every 30,000 inhabitants to the house of representatives. Their pop. was estimated in 1840 as follows: The correctness of this table is doubtful. In the pop. of Honduras is included that of the Mosquito territory, which is under the immediate government of its own chief, and enjoys the protection of Great Britain; while the return relating to Guatimala is made to comprehend the pop. of the British settlement Belize: and to the pop. of Quesaltenango is added that of the prov. of Soconusco on the Pacific, which since 1823 has formed a kind of neutral territory between Central America and Mexico. Don Pedro Molina, the first president of the newly formed federacy, was succeeded in 1824 by Don Manuel Jose Arco, under whose presidency a civil war burst forth between the democratic party in San Salvador headed by General Morazan, and the aristocratic party comprising the clergy and the old Spaniards, who were headed by the president himself. Morazan appears to have exerted himself to the utmost for the maintenance of the general welfare and tranquillity; but the incessant struggle of parties themselves, aided by the appearance of Carrera at the head of his Indian hordes, again brought matters to a crisis, and in 1839, the federal union proclaimed its own dissolution. In the early part of 1842, Carrera captured the city of Guatimala, and Morazan fled to Costarica, where he met a traitor's doom on the 15th of September. On the 7th of October, in the same year, a treaty of alliance offensive and defensive was signed between the states of Guatimala, Honduras, Nicaragua, and San Salvador. In 1845, fresh troubles burst forth in Guatimala and San Salvador; and the federal union of Central A. became avowedly what it had long virtually been,—a political nullity. In 1846, Nicaragua took forcible possession of Greytown-formerly San Juan de Nicaragua—the capital of the Mosquito territory, and held it for some time without advantage to itself, and to the injury of Costarica, to which that port formed the sole outlet on the Atlantic side adapted to its trade with Europe. In 1849, the British government having intimated that "Greytown belongs exclusively to the territory of Mosquito," the Nicaraguan government-which had already entered into a contract with the Pacific Steam navigation company for the purpose of forming a ship-canal through so much of the isthmus of Central A. as appertained to it—repudiated that contract, recalled its minister from London, and under the advice of an American chargé d'affaires, entered into a new contract with some parties in the United States for the execution of a ship-canal, in which the Nicaraguans accorded powers and privileges of an exclusive kind and founded only on their own unwarrantable pretensions to the Mosquito territory. The states who, from their territorial position, ought to have been made parties to any such arrangement, and whose concurrence was necessary, were Mosquito, Costarica, and Nicaragua. The first as possessing the mouth and port of the river St. John; the second as possessing the S bank of the great lake of Nicaragua, and of the river St. John down to the Colorado branch from the main channel; and the third as possessing, from the Machuca rapids, the N bank of the river and lake, as well as the outlet proposed on the Pacific side. Yet Nicaragua alone assumed the right of making this contract, on condition of her sovereignty being established over the territory by the support of the United States. These transactions will, as a matter of course, be repudiated at once by the governments of the United States and of Great Britain. But, at the moment we now write, it is understood that an attempt is making to frame a new federation of the five states of Central A., based on a plan of union, the 13th art. of which declares that "the contracting states repudiate that which has wanted and pretended to call itself the Mosquito monarchy, and all its pretended rights over the port of San Juan and the adjacent territories, which pretended right the government of Great Britain has put forward; and the said contracting states assert the sovereignty of Central A. over all her natural soil, which, before the independence of the country, constituted the Captain-generality of Guatimala; and these states recognise and assert the entire independence of Central A. from all foreign monarchical powers, and repudiate any of their interference or intervention in the affairs and governments of the people of the New World." This article, interpreted according to the usual significance of such documents, amounts to no less than a declaration of war against Great Britain; but that, from the weakness of those states, no such construction will be put upon it by the British government, is no doubt what has been calculated upon in the impudent instigations which dictated it. Two of the states, Guatimala, the largest and most populous, and Costarica, the most energetic and rising among them, have stood aloof from this compact. As regards them, therefore, it appears that the three contracting states do not recognise in them the privilege to dispose of their own soil, but arrogate to themselves the right to dictate in everything connected with the territory of the whole of Central A. The truth is, these small republics of Central A., owing to their constant changes and divisions, can hardly be regarded as regular governments for more than a few years at a time, for revolution succeeds revolution, and change follows change, with a rapidity that defies the pen of history to chronicle with even an approach to exactness; and these continual mobilities and alterations must render their relation to other Powers extremely embarrassing, and particularly to such nations or their subjects as those to whom they may happen to be debtors. CENTRE, a township of Perry co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 36 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 982.--Also a county in the state of Pennsylvania, comprising an area of 1,560 sq. m., bordered on the W by a branch of the Susquehanna, and drained by Bald Eagle and Penn's creeks. The surface is mountainous and rocky, and the soil, except on the streams, indifferent. Pop. in 1830, 18,879; in 1840, 20,492. Cap., Bellefonte.—Also a township of Green co., in the same state. It possesses a hilly surface, with a loamy soil, and is drained by Ten Mile creek and its branches. Pop. in 1840, 1,503. It contains the v. of Clinton.—Also a township of Indiana co., in the same state, drained by Yellow and Twolick creeks, affluents of Blacklick river. It is hilly, and its soil consists of loam and clay. Pop. 1,615.—Also a township of Union co., in the same state, drained by Penn's and Little Mahonaily or Middle creeks, and containing the villages of Middleburg and Centreville. The surface is diversified, and the soil to a considerable extent calcareous loam. Pop. 1,891.—Also a township of Wood co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 98.—Also a township of Morgan co., in the same state. Pop. 1,171.—Also a township of Williams co., in the same state. Pop. 339.—Also a township of Monroe co., in the same state. Pop. 1,740.—Also a township of Mercer co., in the same state. Pop. 159.—Also a township of Guernsey co., in the same state, on the national road, a little to the E of Cambridge. Pop. 1,159.—Also a township of Carroll co., in the same state. Pop. 441. It contains Carrollton, the cap. of the co.—Also a township of Crawford co., in the same state. Pop. 132.—Also a township of Columbiana co., in the same state, intersected by Little Beaver river and the Sandy and Beaver canal. Pop. 3,197. It contains New Lisbon, the cap. of the co.—Also a township of Hancock co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 772.—Also a township of Wayne co., in the same state. Pop. 3,058.—Also a township of Marion co., in the same state.
CENTREBURG, a village of Liberty township, Knox co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., on the S side of the N fork of Licking river, 36 m. NE of Columbus. It consisted in 1840 of about 12 dwellings.

CENTRE (CANAL DU), a canal of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, which connects the Loire with the Saone. It branches from the former near Digoin, and unites with the Saone at Chalons, passing, in its length of 72 m. Paray, Palinge, Saint-Leger, and Chagny. Its summit-level is about 240 ft. above the level of the Loire at Digoin. It has 81 locks; and is 48 ft. in breadth at the surface, and 30 ft. at the bottom. Its depth of water is 5\frac{1}{2} ft. It was completed in 1791, at an expense of £456,000. Its basin is supplied with water to a great extent from the marshes of Longpendee. The principal articles of transport on this canal are grain, wine, brandy, staves, hoops, laths, charcoal, coals, timber, iron, mill-stones, stone, and gypsum.
CENTREFIELD, a village of Canandaigua township, Ontario co., in the state of New York, U. S., 199 m. NW of Albany. It contained in 1840 about 20 dwellings.

CENTRE-HARBOUR, a township of Belknap co., Concord. It presents a diversified surface, bordered on the SE by the Winnipisiogee lake, and on the fertile. CENTREVILLE, a village of Warwick township, Kent co., in the state of Rhode Island, U. S., 11 m. SSE of Providence, on the E side of Pawtuxet river. Pop. 350.—Also a village of Barnstaple township, Barnstaple co., in the state of Massachusetts, 78 m. SE of Boston.—Also a township of Alleghany co., in the state of New York, 265 m. SW of Albany. The surface is diversified; and the soil, consisting of argillaceous mould, is generally fertile. Pop. 1,513. -Also a village of Slippery Rock township, Butler co., in the state of Pennsylvania, consisting of about 60 dwellings.—Also a village of Washington township, Montgomery co., in the state of Ohio, 9 m. SE of Dayton. It is finely situated in a fertile locality, and is built of stone.
