CENTRE WHITE CREEK, a village of White Creek township, Washington co., in the state of New York, U. S., 39 m. NE of Albany, consisting in 1840 of about 30 dwellings.

CENTRON, or CHINTRON, a village of Savoy, in the prov. of Tarentaise, on the r. bank of the Isere, and in the Val de Centron, 4 m. NNE of Moutiers. It is supposed to have been the cap. of the ancient Centrones. The Val de C. extends NE from Moutiers.
CENTURI, a village of the island of Corsica, cant. of Rogliano, on the gulf of San Fiorenza, on the NW coast. It possesses a small port, and exports wine and wood.
CENVES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Monsol, 22 m. NNW of Villefranche-sur-Saône. Pop. 1,370.
CEOS. See ZEA.
CEPAGATTI, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 15 m. S of Città-San- Angelo. Pop. 995. It contains a custom-house.
CEPEDA, a town of Leon, in the prov. and 45 m. S of Salamanca, partido of Sequeros del Condado, in the Sierra de Francia. Pop. 1,116. It contains a church and a custom-house.

CEPHALONI'A, the largest and most populous of the Ionian islands, in N lat. 38° 8′, E long. 21° 18′. It lies 6 m. S of Santa Maura, having Zante on its S extremity, and the island of Ithaca interposed between it and the Morea, from which, towards the entrance of the gulf of Patras, it is about 25 m. distant. Owing to the irregularity of its shape, the precise length and breadth of C. cannot well be defined; but its greatest length is about 31 m.; greatest breadth 28 m.; and circumf. from 160 to 180 m. It has an area of about 348 sq. m.—C. is generally a bare, hilly, stony island, excepting the valleys, many of which are rich and beautiful. A chain of lofty mountains runs throughout the centre of the island, attaining, at its S extremity, the height of about 3,700 ft. From this point, termed the Black mountain—the Mount Enos of antiquity—they gradually decline towards the N, and branch off in lateral directions. Indeed the whole surface of the island may be characterized as rugged and mountainous in the extreme; the elevated portions are arid and barren, presenting nothing to the eye but immense masses of dark grey rock. Between these, however, are a number of small confined valleys filled with rich mould readily admitting of cultivation; and even in the interstices of the rocks, wherever there is a little soil, the vine and olive take root and produce abundantly: so that the agricultural capabilities of the island are much greater than would at first be supposed from the barren and rugged nature of its outline. By recent statistical accounts it appears that of 220,000 acres of surface only 10,000 are devoted to agricultural purposes; about 1,600 are under pasturage; 12,232 under vines; 432 under olives; 6,242 under currants; and about 190,000 are waste and uncultivated. The soil is of great natural fertility; but it is necessary to import considerable quantities of corn from the Morea. The principal productions are raisins, currants, oil, wine-particularly the kind called muscadel-citrons, melons, figs, carobs, pomegranates, salt, and cotton. C. is particularly noted for its raisins, which are of a quality superior to those of the other islands, and even of the Morea, and are raised to the amount of 2,000 or 3,000 tons a-year. The quantity of oil annually made is from 20,000 to 30,000 casks; of wine from 30,000 to 35,000. The Uva passa, or current-vine of C., a low shrubby plant, covers many of the hill-sides nearly to their summits; in other places they are clothed with low brushwood, cistus, heath, and arbutus. This island possesses no stream of sufficient magnitude to deserve the name of a river; but there are several small rills which continue to flow throughout the year, and in winter are swollen by the rains into formidable torrents. It is now almost destitute of timber, an extensive forest which once covered the Black mountain having been burnt down several years ago. - There are no inland lakes or marshes of any extent. During a part of the year, pools are formed in the deep gullies or ravines by the mountain torrents, and these are supposed occasionally to give rise to exhalations unfavourable to health. The principal sources, however, to which the febrile diseases of this island are attributed, are two lagunes communicating with the sea, each terminating in a shallow marshy bay. One of these, the lagune of Livadi, is 8 or 9 m. from Argostoli, and the marsh in which it terminates is 3 m. long by 1 m. broad. The other, the lagune of Cutano, is about 1 m. to the S of the capital, but the extent of the marsh there is by no means so great. Towards the extremity of these lagunes the water is very shallow, and sometimes a large portion of the muddy bottom is left exposed to the action of the sun. Of late years much has been done, by embanking in some parts and deepening in others, to render them less obnoxious. The climate of C. is more variable than that of the other islands; a difference of 24° in the therm. has sometimes been observed in the space of a couple of hours. This may partly arise from the great elevation of the Black mountain, which being generally covered with snow from December to April, must exert a material influence on the temperature of the low grounds. In summer, however, the range of the therm. is higher by several degrees than in the adjacent islands. During winter the prevailing winds are from the NE; in summer from S to E during the early part of the day, and from N to W | during afternoon and evening, with a dead calm from midnight to sunrise. Rain falls at all periods of the year, but most copiously in November, and least in June, July, and August. The pop. of this island in 1833 was 56,447; in 1844, 69,984. The number of scholars in the primary and secondary schools on the island in 1848 was 1,340. Medical men, natives of C., are scattered all over the East. The inhabitants are more industrious agriculturists than their neighbours in Corfu, and carry on manufactures of coarse cotton cloths, particularly of a kind of nankeen used formerly by the Venetians for clothing their troops, and of carpets made of mixed wool and goats' hair. They likewise engage to a great extent in navigation, possessing about 250 small merchant-vessels, which trade to the Levant, Apulia, and other districts bordering on the gulf of Venice. Argostoli, the chief town, has one of the best harbours in the Mediterranean. The imports consist chiefly of corn, woollen cloths, linen, sugar, and hardware.—C. is divided into 4 districts, and sends 7 members to the Ionian legislative assembly, and 1 to the senate.—Argostoli and Lixuri are the chief towns. Santa Eufemia, a small port on the bay of Samos, is the only port on the N side of the island allowed to have communication with the neighbouring continent. - The island, from its mountainous formation, is naturally strong; and its position, with that of the adjoining island of Ithaca, gives it full control over the gulf of Lepanto and the N and W shores of the Morea. This island, the Samos of Homer, and Tetrapolis of Thucydides, is believed to have been much more populous and flourishing in ancient times. It was subject to the Venetians from the year 1449 till the peace of Campo Formio in 1797, when it was ceded to France; during that period it was governed by a proveditor appointed by the republic. It was taken from the French in 1799, and formed, with the other Ionian isles, into an independent commonwealth; but at the peace of Tilsit, in 1807, it was again brought under the dominion of France. In 1809 it was taken by the British, under whose protection it still continues. In 1849 it was the scene of a revolt, which was not put down without bloodshed. See CORFU.
CEPPALONI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato Ultra, district and 11 m. N of Avellino. Pop. 2,500. It has an annual fair.
CEPRANO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the delegation and 8 m. SE of Frosinone, on an affluent of the Sacco or Tolero, on the confines of Naples. Pop. 2,000.
CERA, an island in the Eastern archipelago, in the Banda sea, to the W of the island of Timor Laut, in S lat. 8° 15', W long. 130° 45'. It is about 20 m. in circumference.
CERADINO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 12 m. NW of Verona, on the 1. bank of the Adige.

CERAM, an island in the Eastern archipelago, one of the largest in the Molucca group. It lies between 3° and 4° S lat., and is intersected by the meridian of 130° E. It is above 180 m. in length from E to W; and its average breadth may be taken at 30 m.; its area will therefore approximate to 5,400 sq. m. A chain of high mountains pervades the island in the direction of its length, and is said to attain in some points an alt. of 7,000 ft. above sea-level. It is fertile; and is of all those islands which constitute the native country of the sago-palm, the most distinguished habitat of that important tree, which is here found in immense forests. [Crawfurd.] The wood known in commerce as Amboyna wood, is chiefly the produce of C. The interior is peopled by tribes of Alforas or Horaforas, who, it is reported, form three independent states. They appear to have considerable intercourse both of a commercial and a warlike kind with the coasts of New Guinea [Kolff]; * CER and the Bugis traders from Celebes resort to this island in considerable numbers. The Dutch claim this island, which forms part of the government of Amboyna. They formed a settlement at Wahdi, on the N coast, in 1838, after the abandonment of Fort Du Bois on the SW coast of New Guinea .- Off the E end of the island lie a group of small islands called Ceram-Laut, or Pulo-Ceram, consisting of a larger island encompassed by several smaller islands, and the whole surrounded by an extensive coral reef.
CERAMI, a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 48 m. NW of Catania, district and 7 m. NE of Nicosia. Pop. 3,667. The environs contain mines of silver, copper, salt, and sulphur.

CERAM-LAUT, or LAOUT, a group of islands in the Eastern archipelago, off the SE extremity of the island of Ceram. The largest of the cluster-which consists of three larger and an unknown number of smaller islands—is in N lat. 3° 54', E long. 131° 30'. Spices form the chief produce of these islands, which conduct a considerable trade with Banda, and an almost exclusive trade with New Guinea. Mr. Earl reports that, when at Banda, in 1842, he met with a captain commanding a ship belonging to a Chinese who resided there, and who had lately returned from a voyage to Timor-laut, during which he had found that the S part of Timor-laut consisted of an extensive island, on the N side of which was a bay which afforded excellent anchorage in 8 to 10 fath. throughout the year. He remained there two months, trading with the natives for tortoise-shell, bees'-wax, trepang, &c., and proposed going thither again on the setting-in of the NW monsoon, as stock, yams, Indian corn, &c., were abundant there. This port, which is not 200 m. distant from Port Essington in N Australia, may hereafter be of importance. "I regret very much," Mr. Earl adds, "that we were unable to remain a few days at Timor-laut, since, from the specimens of the natives of this island that I had seen during this voyage, I should have been glad to have had a further acquaintance with them. The young men are partial to emigration, with the view of acquiring sufficient wealth, during the time they remain abroad, to enable them to live in comfort at home, where the knowledge they have acquired during their absence gives them a high standing among their countrymen. We met with a few of these emigrants at the Ki and Arru islands, but their favourite resort is Banda. Here the greater portion enter into the service of the government, or of the residents, in the capacity of police and custom-house peons, nightwatchmen, &c.; while all the prahus sailing out of Banda are manned by them. Others employ themselves in catching fish, cutting timber and fire-wood, and growing vegetables for sale. Indeed the markets are chiefly supplied by them. They are generally good rough carpenters, and upon the whole would make a very good description of labouring settlers for Port-Essington, when such may be required, particularly as there would be little difficulty in obtaining any number of them. They have lately come to Banda in such numbers, that the government have refused to permit any more to settle there."
CERANO, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 9 m. ESE of Novara. Pop. 3,600. It contains some silk spinning-mills.
CERANS-FOULLETOURTE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Pontvalain. Pop. 2,324.
CERAS, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, comarca and 6 m. NNW of Thomar.
CERASO, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 3 m. S of Il Vallo. Pop. 1,000.
CERBATANA, a mountain-ridge of Venezuela, in Guayana, a ramification of the Sierra Parima, in W long. 66° 30'.

CERCADO, a prov. of Peru, in the intendancy of Lima; bounded on the N by the prov. of Chancay; on the E by that of Huarochiri; on the S by the prov. of Canete; and on the W by the Pacific. It is 39 m. in length from N to S, and 24 m. in breadth; and is watered by the Rimac, Lurin, and Carabayllo. Its soil is fertile, and produces grain, fruit, sugar, forage, and legumes, in great abundance. Rain rarely falls, and never except in winter; but a gentle distillation of moisture, locally called garva, is not uncommon. The climate is mild, but insalubrious. Earthquakes are of frequent occurrence. Lima, the cap. of the intendancy, and its port Callao, are situated in this prov.
CERCANCEAU, a village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, 22 m. S of Fontainebleau, on the r. bank of the Bez. Pop. 280. It contains a paper-mill.
CERCEDA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. NNW of Madrid, partido of Colmenar Viejo, on the l. bank of the Manzanares.
CERCHIARA, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Calabria-Citra, district and 11 m. ENE of Castrovillari. Pop. 2,080. It contains 2 churches, and a foundling-hospital.
CERCIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Belleville. Pop. 709. Wine is cultivated in the environs.
CERCLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Verteillac. Pop. 1,364.
CERCOUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Montguyon, 28 m. SE of Jouzac. Pop. 1,824.
CERCY-LA-TOUR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of Fours, on the Canal du Nivernais, 28 m. SE of Nevers. Pop. 1,305.

CERDAGNE, or CERDANA, the ancient district of Cardania, or Ceretania, situated partly in the old French prov. of Roussillon, and in Catalonia in Spain, and now comprised in the French dep. of the Pyrenees-Orientales, and in the new Spanish prov. of Barcelona, Gerona, and Lerida. The name is derived from the Ceretani, its ancient inhabitants. Mont Louis formed the cap. of the French, and Puy- Cerda that of the Spanish section of the district.

CERDON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Poncin, 10 m. SW of Nantua, on the Veyron, at the base of a steep mountain, a ramification of the Jura, and at an alt. of 996 ft. above sea-level. Pop. in 1841, 1,837. It contains some cotton-mills. Fairs are held four times a-year. In the environs are mineral springs, and a coppermine.

CERE, a small river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. and mountains of Cantal; runs E, passing near Vic-en-Carladez and Roquebron; enters the dep. of Lot; passes Bretenoux; and after a total course of 66 m., falls into the Dordogne on the l. bank. The chief articles of transit on this river are staves and faggots.

CERE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lot, arrond. of Figeac. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,582; in 1841, 12,433. The town is 21 m. NNW of Figeac. Pop. in 1841, 3,902. It has 9 annual fairs. Serpentine stone is quarried in the environs.
CEREA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, in the delegation and 20 m. SSE of Verona, on an affluent of the Tartaro, at an alt. of 60 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,000.
CERECEDA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 33 m. NNE of Burgos, and in the partido of Cifuentes. CER
CERECINOS-DE-CAMPOS-O-DE-LOS-BARRIOS, an ancient town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. of Zamora, partido of Benevento. Pop. 860. It contains several churches, and a custom-house.
CEREGNANO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, 5 m. E of Rovigo, between the Canal Bianco and the Adigetto.
CERENCES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Brehal, on the l. bank of the Sienne, 9 m. S of Coutances. Pop. 2,064.
CERENTINO, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, at the NW extremity of the Val Maggia, 5 m. NW of Cevio.

CERENZIA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 25 m. WNW of Cotrone, and 30 m. NNE of Catanzaro, on a mountain, near the r. bank of the Lese, in an insalubrious locality. Pop. 425. Its trade consists chiefly in manna and turpentine.

CERES, a parish of Fifeshire, intersected by the Eden and its tributary the Ceres. Area 8,000 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,944, of whom 1,079 were in the v. of C., which is 21 m. S of Cupar-Fife. The p. also contains the villages of Chance-Inn and Craigrothie.

CERES, a township of M'Kean co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 16 m. NW of Smithport. It has an undulating surface; and is drained by Tunangwant, Sugar, Willow, and Kenjua creeks. The soil consists of gravelly loam. Pop. 625.
CERES, a village of Piedmont, in the prov. and 23 m. NW of Turin, near the junction of the two arms of the Stura. Pop. 2,000.
CERESARA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, 14 m. NW of Mantua, in the district and 7 m. ESE of Castel-Goffredo. Pop. 1,300.

CERESENS, an aboriginal tribe of Senegambia, in the kingdom of Kayor, who inhabit the peninsula terminating in Cape Verd. They speak a peculiar language; recognise no chief; maintain a state of isolation from other tribes; and are vindictive in character, but hospitable withal, and industrious.
CERESO (LAGO). See LUGANO.
